serving.description: 1 serving steamed threadfin (~250g: 180g fillet + 70g ginger-soy sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of steamed threadfin is approximately 250g, comprising 180g of steamed fillet and 70g of ginger-soy sauce, yielding approximately 295 kcal, 55g protein, and 8g fat per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Threadfin (Ikan Senangin / Polynemus spp.) — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for Polynemus spp. raw fish values
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values represent the composite dish (180g steamed threadfin fillet + 70g ginger-soy sauce per 250g serving). Fish nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo for local threadfin (senangin/kurau). Micronutrient values (selenium, vitamin D, omega-3 profile) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for Polynemus spp. and related Polynemidae species. Sodium elevated from raw fish baseline (~85mg/100g) to ~350mg/100g in the composite dish due to soy sauce contribution in the sauce component.

gi_source.title: N/A — Steamed threadfin with ginger-soy sauce contains negligible carbohydrates (0.8g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (consulted; no GI value applicable for very-low-carbohydrate fish dish)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed threadfin is a high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate fish dish (0.8g carbohydrate per 100g). Glycemic index is not applicable as the food contains negligible carbohydrates. GL per 250g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = not applicable; reported as 0.0 to indicate negligible glycemic impact. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI value assigned for fish dishes with negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 350mg/100g in composite dish (875mg per 250g serving) due to soy sauce; offset by omega-3 antihypertensive benefit and potassium 360mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should be aware that a 250g serving delivers approximately 875mg sodium (predominantly from ginger-soy sauce); requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation can lower sodium to approximately 200–300mg per serving, while the omega-3 content (~2.5g DHA+EPA per serving) provides a clinically meaningful antihypertensive benefit of approximately 2–4 mmHg systolic reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.8g/100g; 2g per serving), very high protein (22g/100g), and high vitamin D (7.5mcg/100g = 50% daily AI) supporting insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed threadfin is an excellent protein source for people with Type 2 Diabetes, providing negligible carbohydrate load (2g per 250g serving), 55g high-quality protein for satiety and glycemic stability, and 18.75mcg vitamin D per serving which supports insulin sensitivity; the primary dietary consideration is sodium from the soy sauce component.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 DHA+EPA (~2.5g per 250g serving), low saturated fat (0.8g/100g), selenium 42mcg/100g antioxidant protection, and lean protein profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed threadfin is cardioprotective: a 250g serving provides approximately 2.5g omega-3 DHA+EPA (exceeding the AHA recommendation of 1g/day for CVD patients), low saturated fat at 0.8g/100g, selenium 42mcg/100g (76% daily AI) for antioxidant protection, and 65mg/100g cholesterol within acceptable limits for a lean fish; sodium management via sauce reduction is recommended for patients with concurrent heart failure or fluid retention.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 DHA+EPA ~2.5g per serving exceeds evidence-based anti-inflammatory threshold of 2g/day; selenium 42mcg/100g provides additional anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steamed threadfin is one of the highest-value anti-inflammatory fish choices available in Singapore, providing ~2.5g DHA+EPA per serving (exceeding the evidence-based RA anti-inflammatory dose of >2g/day) and 42mcg/100g selenium (76% daily AI), making it a premium dietary recommendation for RA patients alongside or in preference to mackerel.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Threadfin (Ikan Senangin / 清蒸马友鱼) is one of the most prized fish dishes in Singapore and Malaysian Chinese cuisine, prepared by steaming a whole threadfin fillet (Polynemus spp., commonly Eleutheronema tetradactylum) over a bed of ginger slices and finishing with hot oil and a ginger-soy sauce poured over the fish. The flesh is exceptionally delicate, silky, and moist with a mild, sweet flavour and fine flake texture that distinguishes threadfin from other steamed fish. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 250g (180g fillet + 70g sauce), delivering approximately 295 kcal, 55g protein (very high), 8g fat including approximately 2.5g omega-3 DHA+EPA, and only 2g carbohydrate. Sodium is approximately 875mg per serving, predominantly from the soy sauce component. Threadfin is exceptionally rich in vitamin D (7.5mcg/100g = 50% daily AI per 100g), selenium (42mcg/100g = 76% daily AI), vitamin B12 (3.5mcg/100g), and niacin (5.5mg/100g). The fish is widely marketed in Singapore wet markets and supermarkets as 'Indian Salmon' — a commercial misnomer, as threadfin belongs to the Polynemidae family and is entirely unrelated to Salmonidae. It is a premium confinement food in Chinese postpartum tradition due to its DHA content, and is a staple of Teochew and Cantonese zi char menus. Market price ranges from SGD 20–50/kg whole fish. Clinically, steamed threadfin is excellent for Type 2 Diabetes (negligible carbohydrate, high protein, high vitamin D), cardiovascular disease (omega-3 >2.5g/serving, low saturated fat), and rheumatoid arthritis (anti-inflammatory omega-3 dose). Sodium management via sauce reduction is the primary dietary consideration for hypertension patients.

threadfin senangin steamed: Indian salmon steamed (misnomer — not a salmon species)

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for Polynemus spp. micronutrient profile. The composite dish values (fillet + sauce) are well-supported by the approved source combination. Confidence is high for macros, sodium, and key micronutrients (vitamin D, selenium, B12). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for Polynemus spp. and are consistent with known fatty acid profiles for Polynemidae species.
notes: 1. COMPOSITE DISH: Per-100g values represent the composite dish (180g steamed threadfin fillet + 70g ginger-soy sauce per 250g serving). Sodium is elevated from raw fish baseline (~85mg/100g) to 350mg/100g in the composite due to soy sauce. 2. OMEGA-3: Polyunsaturated fat (1.4g/100g) is predominantly DHA+EPA; approximately 1.0g DHA+EPA per 100g (2.5g per 250g serving) based on USDA SR Legacy fatty acid data for Polynemus spp. 3. DIETARY FIBRE: dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a pure fish and sauce dish with no plant fibre components. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for fresh marine fish. 4. WATER: water_g set to -1: moisture data for the composite steamed threadfin dish (fillet + sauce) is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-14; the composite dish preparation (fillet plus sauce) means a single reliable water_g figure cannot be derived from either approved source without direct laboratory measurement. 5. GI: Steamed threadfin; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.8g/100g; 2g per 250g serving). gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. GL per serving reported as 0.0 to indicate negligible glycemic impact. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI value assigned for fish dishes with negligible carbohydrate content. 6. MISNOMER NOTE: Threadfin (Polynemus spp.) is widely sold in Singapore as 'Indian Salmon' — this is a commercial misnomer. Threadfin belongs to family Polynemidae and is not related to Salmonidae. 7. ONTOLOGY: No exact FoodOn URI or FoodEx2 code exists for steamed threadfin dish; closest parent matches documented with honest gap notes. 8. MONOUNSATURATED/POLYUNSATURATED FAT: Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy for Polynemus spp. raw fish, adjusted for steaming (minimal fat change). These are best-available estimates from an approved source.
